methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $300M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $350M - $10.0B.

private.name: 1X Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $10B

private.name: Bedrock Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.75B

private.name: Dexterity
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.65B

private.name: Collaborative Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $500M+

private.name: Fairmat
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $350M

data.narrative:
GrayMatter Robotics, founded by Ariyan Kabir and Brual Shah, is a Series B robotics and manufacturing company currently valued at $300M post-money, having raised $70M+ to date. The valuation framework draws on a peer universe of nine comparable companies—one public and eight private—in the same sector and stage.

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis uses the $300M last post-money as a base and applies a typical 1.9x Series B step-up multiplier, implying a valuation of $570M. Peer-median analysis examines five same-stage comparables in Robotics & Manufacturing: 1X Technologies ($10.0B), Bedrock Robotics ($1.8B), Dexterity ($1.6B), Collaborative Robotics ($500M), and Fairmat ($350M). The peer group median is $1.6B, with a range spanning $350M to $10.0B.

These two methods triangulate to a valuation range of $484M (low) to $1.9B (high), with a median of $1.1B. The current $300M+ valuation sits below this range, reflecting stage-progression expectations and material variation within the peer set. Key uncertainties include the significant spread between peer valuations—driven by differences in revenue, growth trajectory, and technology maturity—and the limited weight of public comparable data. The median of $1.1B represents the most balanced estimate given the available evidence.
